main_summary.summary: Euvester Simpson, interviewed in Jackson, Mississippi on March 11, 2013 by historian John Dittmer, recounts her path from the Mississippi Delta into SNCC and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. Born in Itta Bena and raised between Greenwood and a sharecropping plantation, she attended Amanda Elzy High School, then moved to Racine, Wisconsin at age 14 to live with her sister. Returning to Mississippi in January 1963, she attended a mass meeting in Greenwood where she met Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, and her lifelong friend June Johnson. Recruited by Annelle Ponder for SCLC's Citizenship School in Charleston, South Carolina, she studied under Septima Clark, Myles Horton, and Esau Jenkins. On the return bus trip in June 1963, Simpson, Ponder, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and others were arrested in Winona, Mississippi; she shared a cell with Hamer during the beatings and learned of Medgar Evers' assassination upon release. She worked the WATS line at the COFO office in Jackson during Freedom Summer 1964, attended the Atlantic City convention with the MFDP delegation, then enrolled at Tougaloo College. She later worked for the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, Friends of Children of Mississippi, and the Mid-South Delta Initiative.
key_themes: Mississippi Delta sharecropping and family land loss
Winona, Mississippi jailhouse beatings of June 1963
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and Atlantic City challenge
Tougaloo College and post-Movement community development
main_summary.historical_significance:
Simpson is one of the few surviving direct witnesses to the Winona jail beatings of Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, and June Johnson — events Hamer would describe at the 1964 Democratic National Convention. Her cell-by-cell account, including the gospel song 'Walk With Me' that the women sang to calm Hamer through the night, provides a first-person source on a foundational MFDP incident.

The interview also documents the institutional infrastructure of Freedom Summer from inside the Jackson COFO office: the WATS-line shift system that connected field workers to legal and medical support, the personnel mix of SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and NAACP staff who shared the space, and the post-Atlantic City rift between rural activists and those willing to accept the two-seat compromise. Simpson's later career — Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights, Friends of Children of Mississippi Head Start, the Kellogg Foundation's Mid-South Delta Initiative, and Hurricane Katrina case work — traces how Movement-era networks carried into Mississippi's community-development sector across five decades.

chapters.title: Delta Origins and Family Land
chapters.summary: Simpson describes her birth in Itta Bena, Mississippi as the youngest of seven, her father's work as a butcher and her parents' sharecropping years between Greenwood and a plantation. She recounts her mother's family in Mound Bayou, her great-grandfather Jeff Davis Pearson who attended Tougaloo College in the first generation after emancipation, and how the family lost their land in the 1920s-30s.

chapters.title: Schooling in the Delta
chapters.summary: Simpson recounts attending Ellis Rogers Elementary on the Mississippi Valley State University campus, then seventh through ninth grade in Itta Bena, and tenth grade at the newly consolidated Amanda Elzy High School built to bus Black students from across the county. The split session calendar allowed students to chop and pick cotton.

chapters.title: Emmett Till and Reverend George Lee
chapters.summary: Simpson, age nine when Emmett Till was lynched in 1955, describes seeing the open-casket photograph in Jet magazine and her terror that any Black child could be next. She recounts her father's NAACP membership and his attendance at the funeral of Reverend George Lee, a voting-rights activist gunned down in Belzoni.

chapters.title: Racine, Wisconsin and Cultural Shock
chapters.summary: At 14, Simpson moved to Racine, Wisconsin to live with her sister and attend Washington Park High School, where she encountered an integrated classroom for the first time. She joined the NAACP Youth Chapter there and tried to organize a Mississippi support trip. She returned to Mississippi for her senior year to graduate from Amanda Elzy in January 1963.

chapters.title: Movement Entry in Greenwood
chapters.summary: Returning to Mississippi in January 1963, Simpson attended a SNCC mass meeting in Greenwood where she met Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, Mary Lane, and her lifelong friend June Johnson. She describes her father calling Guyot to put him personally in charge of her safety, and her own commitment to voter-registration canvassing in Greenwood and Itta Bena.

chapters.title: SCLC Citizenship School in Charleston
chapters.summary: Annelle Ponder of SCLC selected Simpson, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and others for the two-week Citizenship School in Charleston, South Carolina. Simpson studied with Myles Horton of Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins, and Guy and Candie Carawan, learning the rights and responsibilities of citizenship and how to conduct voter-registration workshops back home.

chapters.title: Arrest and Beatings in Winona
chapters.summary: On the bus trip back from Charleston in June 1963, the group was arrested at the Winona, Mississippi bus station after using the white side of the waiting room. Simpson describes the jailhouse cell-by-cell beatings of Ponder, Johnson, Hamer, and herself with a leather strap, the trustees forced to administer them, and singing 'Walk With Me' through the night with Hamer to keep her conscious as she developed a fever from her injuries.
related_events: Winona, Mississippi arrest and beatings of June 1963

chapters.title: Medgar Evers and the Oxford Trial
chapters.summary: Andrew Young arrived to release the group from Winona on Wednesday and informed them that Medgar Evers had been assassinated the night before in Jackson. Simpson later testified at the federal trial in Oxford, Mississippi, where she was given only a few minutes on the stand and not permitted to describe what she had heard and seen happen to the others.

chapters.title: Freedom Vote and Summer Orientation
chapters.summary: Simpson conducted Freedom Vote workshops in Clarksdale at the church of Aaron E. Henry, the MFDP candidate for governor, demonstrating mock voting procedures. She attended part of the Freedom Summer orientation in Oxford, Ohio in 1964 but left after a few days when her mother fell ill. Word arrived that James Chaney, Michael 'Mickey' Schwerner, and Andrew Goodman had disappeared in Meridian.
related_events: Disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman

chapters.title: COFO Office and the WATS Line
chapters.summary: Through summer 1964 Simpson worked shifts on the WATS line at the COFO office in Jackson — a 24-hour staffed phone that field workers across Mississippi used to check in and report trouble. The office held SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and NAACP staff together; Simpson met Bob Moses, Dave Dennis, Dona Richards Moses, Gwen Gillon, Worth Long, and her future husband Jesse Moore there.

chapters.title: MFDP at Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Simpson rode the bus to Atlantic City for the 1964 Democratic National Convention, sharing delegation badges to rotate observers into the hall. She witnessed the LBJ press conference timed to interrupt Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony and the internal rift when Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray rejected the two-seats-at-large compromise. Simpson sided with the rural activists who refused the offer.

chapters.title: Tougaloo College Years
chapters.summary: In fall 1964 Simpson enrolled at Tougaloo College on a Movement-arranged scholarship alongside Gwen Gillon, Hollis Watkins, and MacArthur Cotton. She recalls Dr. Naomi Townsend's strict English class — where Simpson's afro and slacks drew rebuke — and Dr. Ernst Borinski's internationally known forums. She married Jesse Moore in early 1965 and began raising five children, attending Tougaloo for two years.

chapters.title: Career in Civil Rights and Community Development
chapters.summary: Simpson worked as a legal secretary for the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, then at Friends of Children of Mississippi Head Start under director Fred Mangram Thomas, then managed a Voice of Calvary health clinic in Mendenhall. At Delta State she ran three AmeriCorps programs, then served as a Mid-South Delta Initiative community coach for the Kellogg Foundation, leading Mississippi-Louisiana-Arkansas delegations on three trips to Ghana.
keywords: Lawyers Committee for Civil Rights Under Law

chapters.title: Reflections and Family
chapters.summary: Simpson assesses Mississippi's surface and structural changes: residential integration and Black elected officials versus eroded tax bases, 90-percent-Black public schools, and the rise of Black-on-Black violence absent from her Delta childhood. She closes by introducing her five children — Earl, Orlando, Omar, T., and attorney Jessica — and her second husband Les.
